options.a: A researcher deeply invested in their own project can genuinely, if unintentionally, develop blind spots about that project's potential ethical concerns, simply from being close to the work and motivated to see it succeed — an independent reviewer, with no comparable personal investment in the project's success, can bring a genuinely different, less biased perspective and is more likely to notice a concern the original researcher's own closeness to the work caused them to overlook, echoing the same value of independent verification already established generally throughout this course's emphasis on reproducibility and external checking
options.b: This concept has no relationship to the data consent or dual-use disclosure considerations already covered earlier in this course
options.c: A researcher's own careful self-assessment of a project's ethical implications is always equally thorough and equally reliable as an independent review by someone not directly involved in the project
options.d: Independent ethical review is only ever necessary for projects that the original researcher already personally believes have genuine ethical problems, making it unnecessary for any project the researcher already considers ethically sound

theory.text: This extends the research ethics topic by applying the same independent-verification value already established throughout this course's reproducibility and statistical rigor content to ethical review specifically. A researcher deeply invested in their own project's success can develop genuine, unintentional blind spots about that project's potential ethical concerns — not through any bad faith, but simply because sustained closeness to one's own work and genuine motivation to see it succeed can make it harder to notice issues an outside perspective would catch more readily. An independent reviewer, without that same personal investment in the project succeeding, brings a genuinely different vantage point and is more likely to notice a concern the original researcher's own proximity to the work caused them to overlook entirely — the same underlying principle behind why reproducibility, peer review, and independent verification matter throughout scientific practice generally, here applied specifically to catching ethical blind spots before a project actually reaches real customers, not just methodological errors before a project reaches other researchers.

question: Andina Retail Group's AI researcher documents a clear plan for what to do if a deployed research technique is later discovered to have caused genuine, unanticipated harm — including who should be notified, how quickly, and what corrective action would be taken — before the technique is ever actually deployed, rather than only figuring out a response plan after a genuine problem has already occurred. Why does preparing this kind of response plan in advance reflect sound research ethics practice?

options.a: This concept has no relationship to the proactive incident-preparedness principles already covered elsewhere on this training platform
options.b: Deciding how to respond to a genuine problem calmly and deliberately in advance, before any actual harm has occurred and before the pressure and urgency of a real live incident, generally produces a more thoughtful, more genuinely protective response plan than trying to improvise an appropriate response for the first time while already in the middle of an actual, unfolding crisis — echoing the same proactive-versus-reactive incident-preparedness principle already established generally in this platform's Data Engineering and Supply Chain courses, here applied specifically to the ethical responsibility of responding to unanticipated harm from a deployed research technique
options.c: A response plan for genuine unanticipated harm should always be figured out for the first time only after an actual problem has already occurred, since preparing one in advance provides no genuine benefit over improvising a response during an actual live incident
options.d: Research techniques that have been carefully and rigorously developed following genuine scientific methodology can never actually cause any genuine, unanticipated harm once deployed, making advance response planning an unnecessary precaution

theory.text: This closes the entire research ethics topic by extending the proactive-versus-reactive incident-preparedness principle already established generally in this platform's Data Engineering course (the risk-register and disruption-simulation content) and Supply Chain course to the specific ethical responsibility of responding to unanticipated harm from a deployed AI research technique. However carefully a technique has been developed and validated, genuine unanticipated real-world harm remains a real possibility once something is actually deployed to affect real customer-facing decisions — no amount of careful research methodology can guarantee this risk away entirely. Deciding calmly and deliberately, in advance, exactly how to respond if this does happen — who gets notified, how quickly, what corrective action follows — produces a genuinely more thoughtful, more protective response plan than trying to improvise an appropriate response for the first time while already under the real pressure and urgency of an actual, unfolding incident, exactly the same proactive-preparedness discipline this training platform has consistently emphasized across supply chain disruptions, financial stress scenarios, and now the specific ethical responsibility a research team carries once its work actually affects real people.

question: Andina Retail Group's AI researcher, when presenting a research finding to company leadership, explicitly states the finding's genuine limitations and the specific conditions under which it might not hold, rather than presenting only the positive result with full confidence and no caveats. Why does including genuine limitations in a research communication, even to a non-technical audience, reflect sound and ultimately more trustworthy communication practice?

options.a: A research finding's genuine limitations are only relevant to a technical audience and have no bearing on how a non-technical business leadership audience should actually interpret or act on the finding
options.b: This concept has no relationship to the audience-tailored communication principle already covered earlier in this course
options.c: Non-technical business leadership audiences should never be told about a research finding's genuine limitations, since disclosing any caveat always makes a finding seem completely worthless and not worth acting on at all
options.d: Presenting a finding with its genuine limitations honestly disclosed, even to a non-technical audience who might not fully grasp every technical nuance, gives leadership a more accurate basis for deciding how much weight to actually place on the finding and what risks a resulting business decision might carry — omitting genuine limitations to make a finding sound more impressive or more certain than it actually is, even with good intentions, ultimately risks leadership making a decision based on an incomplete, overconfident picture of what the research actually supports

theory.text: This extends the audience-tailored communication principle from air-016 with a genuinely important honesty discipline that applies regardless of which audience is being addressed. Tailoring the level of technical detail to an audience's actual needs (per air-016) is genuinely different from selectively omitting a finding's real limitations simply because they might complicate an otherwise clean, impressive-sounding story — even a non-technical leadership audience, who might not grasp every technical nuance of why a limitation exists, still genuinely deserves to know that the limitation exists at all, since that information directly affects how much confidence they should reasonably place in the finding and what risk a resulting business decision built on it might actually carry. Omitting genuine limitations to make a finding sound more impressive or more certain than the underlying research actually supports, even without any deliberately dishonest intent, ultimately risks leadership making a real business decision based on an incomplete, overconfident picture — precisely the kind of honest, appropriately humble communication this entire course's statistical rigor and reproducibility content has been building toward, now extended to how findings are actually presented and acted upon.

question: Andina Retail Group's AI researcher uses a clear, well-labeled data visualization (per this training platform's dedicated data visualization guidance) rather than a dense table of raw numbers when presenting a research finding's key result to company leadership. Why does thoughtful visualization genuinely matter for research communication, beyond simply being a more polished presentation choice?

options.a: A dense table of raw numbers always conveys a research finding's key pattern or trend exactly as clearly and immediately as a well-designed visualization, making thoughtful visualization an unnecessary additional effort with no genuine communication benefit
options.b: Data visualization exists purely for aesthetic, decorative purposes with no genuine connection to how quickly or accurately an audience actually understands the underlying finding
options.c: A well-designed visualization can convey a genuine pattern, trend, or comparison far more immediately and intuitively than a dense table of raw numbers requires a reader to manually parse and mentally compare themselves — for an audience that needs to grasp a finding's practical implication quickly (like busy leadership making a real decision), thoughtful visualization genuinely reduces the cognitive effort required to correctly understand what the data actually shows, directly connecting to the cognitive-load management principle already covered in this platform's Artificial Cognitive Systems course
options.d: This concept has no relationship to the cognitive load management principle already covered in this platform's Artificial Cognitive Systems course

theory.text: This closes the entire AI Research Methods course — and the entire Artificial Intelligence course sequence on this training platform — by connecting effective research communication directly to the cognitive load management principle already established in this platform's Artificial Cognitive Systems course (cog-017). A dense table of raw numbers technically contains all the same underlying information as a well-designed visualization, but it requires a reader to do real, active cognitive work parsing and mentally comparing those numbers themselves to actually extract the pattern or trend being communicated — exactly the kind of avoidable cognitive burden the Artificial Cognitive Systems course cautioned against imposing unnecessarily on a limited-capacity human audience. A thoughtful visualization instead does much of that pattern-extraction work upfront, letting a busy leadership audience grasp a finding's practical implication quickly and accurately with far less required mental effort — closing this entire course, and this training platform's whole Artificial Intelligence sequence, with the same consistent, business-grounded theme: genuinely rigorous, technically sound AI research and findings only create real business value once they're communicated in a way their actual intended audience can genuinely understand and confidently act on.

question: Andina Retail Group's AI researcher, before starting a new research project, writes a short 'scoping statement' explicitly listing what the research will NOT attempt to address, alongside the research question itself. Why is explicitly documenting a project's out-of-scope boundaries a genuinely useful discipline, connecting to the requirements-scoping practice already covered in this platform's Data Architecture course?

options.a: A scoping statement listing what a project will not address has no relationship to the requirements-scoping practice already covered in this platform's Data Architecture course, making the comparison invalid
options.b: A research project's scope should always be left completely open-ended and undefined, since narrowing it in advance to a specific question always limits the value of any findings produced
options.c: Explicitly naming what a research project will not attempt to address, mirroring the requirements-scoping discipline already established in this platform's Data Architecture course, prevents the project's actual research question from silently expanding mid-project to cover every tangentially related idea that comes up — an unscoped project risks never reaching a clear, testable conclusion because its target keeps shifting, while a project with an explicit out-of-scope boundary can be judged as genuinely complete once its original, narrower question is actually answered
options.d: Explicitly documenting out-of-scope boundaries means the researcher has already decided the excluded topics are unimportant forever, rather than simply outside this particular project's current focus

theory.text: This extends the precise research-question formulation from air-001 and air-017/air-018 by connecting it directly to the requirements-scoping discipline already established in this platform's Data Architecture course, where clearly bounding a project's scope up front was shown to prevent uncontrolled scope creep. A research project without an explicit out-of-scope boundary is vulnerable to the same failure mode: its target quietly expands mid-project to chase every tangentially related idea that surfaces along the way, meaning the project may never reach a clear, testable conclusion because the question it's actually trying to answer keeps shifting underneath it. Writing a short scoping statement — not just what the research asks, but explicitly what it deliberately leaves out — gives the project a genuine, checkable definition of done: it's complete once its original, narrower question is actually answered, not once every possible related curiosity has been satisfied.

question: Andina Retail Group's AI researcher, when formulating a new research question, explicitly identifies the specific business stakeholder who would actually use the answer and what decision that stakeholder would make differently depending on the result. Why does tying a research question to a genuine downstream decision, rather than pursuing an interesting question in isolation, matter for research prioritization at a company like Andina Retail Group?

options.a: Every research question pursued by a company research team must always be tied to an identifiable immediate business decision, with no legitimate place for any exploratory research that doesn't have an immediate practical application
options.b: This concept has no relationship to the falsifiability or scoping considerations already covered earlier in this topic
options.c: Identifying which stakeholder would use a research answer has no bearing on how a company should prioritize its genuinely limited research effort and resources
options.d: A research question tied to a genuine, identifiable downstream decision has a clear test of practical value — if the answer wouldn't actually change what any real stakeholder does, the research may be intellectually interesting but doesn't yet justify the company's limited research resources ahead of work that would directly inform an actual business decision; this doesn't mean purely exploratory research never has value, but it does mean deliberately tying applied research to a genuine decision-maker helps prioritize limited effort toward what will actually get used

theory.text: This closes the research-problem-formulation topic by adding a practical prioritization lens on top of the falsifiability (air-018) and scoping (air-033) criteria already established. Tying a candidate research question to a specific, identifiable stakeholder and the concrete decision that stakeholder would actually make differently based on the answer gives a genuine, checkable test of practical value ahead of committing limited company research resources — if no real decision would actually change regardless of the answer, the question may still be intellectually worthwhile, but it doesn't automatically justify priority ahead of work that would directly inform something a real person at Andina Retail Group is genuinely about to decide. This is a prioritization filter for applied research specifically, not a blanket claim that every worthwhile research question must have an immediate practical application — but for a company research team with genuinely finite capacity, it's a sound, honest way to decide what to work on first.

question: Andina Retail Group's AI researcher keeps a running, structured log of every source reviewed during a literature review — including sources that turned out to be irrelevant or unhelpful — rather than only keeping notes on the sources that ended up being cited in the final research summary. Why is logging the irrelevant or unhelpful sources, not just the useful ones, a genuinely sound practice?

options.a: Sources that turn out to be irrelevant or unhelpful during a literature review should always be immediately discarded with no record kept, since they provide no genuine value to document in any form
options.b: A literature review log containing only the sources that ended up cited in the final summary is always exactly as useful and complete as one that also logs sources reviewed and found unhelpful
options.c: A structured log covering every source actually reviewed, including ones that turned out irrelevant, prevents the researcher (or a future colleague continuing the same line of inquiry) from wastefully re-reviewing the exact same unhelpful source a second time, and provides honest documentation of how thorough the search actually was — a log containing only the sources that happened to be useful can't be distinguished from a search that simply got lucky versus one that was genuinely comprehensive
options.d: This concept has no relationship to the systematic literature review process or citation-signal triage already covered earlier in this course

theory.text: This extends the systematic literature review discipline from air-003 and the citation-signal triage from air-019 with a practical record-keeping habit. Logging every source actually reviewed — including the ones that turned out irrelevant or unhelpful, not only the ones that made it into the final cited summary — serves two genuine purposes: it prevents the same researcher, or a colleague picking up the same line of inquiry later, from wastefully re-reviewing a source already known to be unhelpful, and it provides honest, checkable documentation of how thorough the search actually was. A log containing only the sources that happened to prove useful can't be distinguished, after the fact, from a search that simply got lucky finding good sources quickly versus one that was genuinely comprehensive and systematic — the full log, including the dead ends, is what actually demonstrates genuine thoroughness.

question: Andina Retail Group's AI researcher, while conducting a literature review, deliberately seeks out at least one source that argues against or complicates the position the researcher personally expects to find supported. Why does deliberately seeking out this kind of disconfirming or complicating source matter for a genuinely thorough literature review, connecting to the confirmation-bias concern already raised in this topic?

options.a: A thorough literature review should only ever include sources that support the researcher's own expected position, since including any disconfirming or complicating source would make the review less useful
options.b: Deliberately seeking out disconfirming sources during a literature review has no relationship to the confirmation-bias concern already raised earlier in this course
options.c: Sources that argue against or complicate a researcher's expected position are always methodologically weaker than sources that happen to confirm it, making them safe to exclude from any genuinely thorough review
options.d: A literature review conducted only by searching for sources that confirm what the researcher already expects to find risks the exact same confirmation bias already flagged in air-003 and air-017, just expressed through selective search rather than selective interpretation of results — deliberately seeking out sources that argue against or complicate the expected position forces genuine engagement with the strongest available counter-evidence, producing a more balanced, more honest picture of what the existing literature actually says rather than a one-sided summary that happens to support what the researcher wanted to find from the start

theory.text: This closes the literature-review-methods topic by directly connecting to the confirmation-bias concern already raised for informal literature searches in air-003 and for null-hypothesis framing in air-017 — here applied specifically to how a literature search itself is conducted, not just how results get interpreted. A search conducted only by looking for sources that confirm an already-expected position risks producing a one-sided, artificially clean-looking summary of 'what the literature says' that actually just reflects what the researcher was looking for from the start. Deliberately seeking out at least one source that argues against or meaningfully complicates that expected position forces genuine engagement with the strongest available counter-evidence, which produces an honestly more balanced picture of the actual state of existing research — and, practically, makes the researcher's own eventual position considerably more defensible, since it will already have grappled with the best available objections rather than being caught off guard by them later.

question: Andina Retail Group's AI researcher runs a 'pilot experiment' on a small subset of data before committing to the full-scale experimental run, specifically to catch obvious setup problems early and inexpensively. Why does running a small, cheap pilot before the full experiment reflect sound experimental practice, connecting to the sanity-check-baseline reasoning already established in this topic?

options.a: A pilot experiment's only genuine purpose is to produce the final, publishable result, with no role in catching setup errors before the full-scale run
options.b: A pilot experiment run on a small subset of data is far cheaper and faster to run than the full-scale experiment, which means an obvious setup problem (a bug, a misconfigured pipeline, an unreasonable-looking result) can be caught and fixed while the cost of discovering it is still low — echoing the same early-and-cheap error-catching logic already established for sanity-check baselines in air-021, just applied to the overall experimental scale rather than to the specific comparison being tested
options.c: Running a pilot experiment before a full-scale run has no relationship to the sanity-check-baseline reasoning already established earlier in this topic
options.d: A pilot experiment run on a small subset of data always produces results that are exactly as statistically reliable and generalizable as the full-scale experimental run, making the full-scale run unnecessary once a pilot has been completed

theory.text: This extends the sanity-check-baseline reasoning from air-021 with a closely related, genuinely practical experimental habit: catching problems cheaply, early, before committing significant resources. A small-scale pilot experiment costs far less time and compute than the full-scale run, meaning an obvious setup problem — a bug in the data pipeline, a misconfigured evaluation metric, a result so far outside reasonable expectations it signals something is broken — can be caught and fixed while the cost of discovering it is still low, exactly the same early-and-cheap error-catching logic already established for sanity-check baselines, just applied to the overall experimental scale rather than to a specific weak comparison technique. A pilot isn't a substitute for the full-scale experiment's statistical reliability — a small subset generally can't support the same confidence as the full run — but it's a genuinely sound, low-cost first checkpoint before investing in the complete, expensive experimental commitment.

question: Andina Retail Group's AI researcher, when designing an experiment to compare a new technique against a baseline, ensures both the new technique and the baseline are given access to exactly the same computational budget (same amount of training time, same hardware) during the comparison. Why does this kind of matched computational budget matter for a genuinely fair experimental comparison?

options.a: A new technique given a meaningfully larger computational budget than the baseline will always perform identically to a baseline given a much smaller budget, making budget matching irrelevant to a fair comparison
options.b: Computational budget matching only matters for comparisons between different neural network architectures, with no relevance to any other kind of AI technique comparison
options.c: If a new technique is given a meaningfully larger computational budget than the baseline during a comparison, any observed improvement might simply reflect the extra compute rather than a genuine advantage of the new technique itself over the baseline — a fair, informative comparison requires holding the computational budget roughly matched between the new technique and the baseline, isolating whether the technique itself, not just extra resources, is what's actually producing the improvement, the same kind of confound-control reasoning already applied to data leakage in air-022
options.d: This concept has no relationship to the data leakage or sanity-check-baseline confound-control reasoning already covered earlier in this topic

theory.text: This closes the entire experimental-design-ai topic by adding a further confound-control discipline alongside the data-leakage check from air-022 and the sanity-check baseline from air-021 — all three address different ways an experimental comparison can be quietly unfair or misleading. If a new technique receives a meaningfully larger computational budget than the baseline it's being compared against, any observed improvement becomes genuinely ambiguous: does it reflect a real advantage of the new technique itself, or simply the fact that it was given more resources to work with, an advantage the baseline never had a fair chance to also benefit from? Holding the computational budget roughly matched between the new technique and the baseline isolates the comparison to what actually matters — whether the technique itself, not just extra compute thrown at the problem, is what's producing the improvement — the same underlying discipline of controlling for confounding factors that already motivated checking rigorously for data leakage.

question: Andina Retail Group's AI researcher evaluates a new technique on more than one benchmark dataset, rather than reporting results from only a single benchmark, before concluding the technique represents a genuine, general improvement. Why does evaluating across multiple benchmarks, not just one, matter for drawing a genuinely reliable conclusion?

options.a: A technique that performs well on a single specific benchmark might be capturing something genuinely useful, or might simply be well-suited to quirks specific to that one benchmark's particular data distribution or construction — evaluating across multiple, meaningfully different benchmarks tests whether an improvement genuinely generalizes beyond one dataset's specific characteristics, giving considerably more confidence that the finding reflects a real, broadly useful improvement rather than an artifact of one particular benchmark's own idiosyncrasies
options.b: This concept has no relationship to the benchmark-bias concerns already covered earlier in this topic
options.c: A technique's performance on a single benchmark dataset always perfectly and reliably predicts its performance on every other possible benchmark dataset, making evaluation across multiple benchmarks an unnecessary additional step
options.d: Evaluating a new technique across multiple different benchmark datasets has no relationship to whether a finding genuinely generalizes beyond one specific dataset's own particular characteristics

theory.text: This extends the benchmark-bias concerns from air-024 with a related, genuinely important generalization check. A technique that performs impressively well on one single specific benchmark might genuinely be capturing something broadly useful — or it might simply be particularly well-matched to quirks specific to that one benchmark's own data distribution or construction, in which case the strong result wouldn't actually reflect a genuine, general improvement at all. Evaluating across multiple, meaningfully different benchmarks — ideally constructed through different collection processes, representing somewhat different populations or task variations — tests directly whether an observed improvement actually holds up beyond one dataset's specific characteristics, giving considerably more confidence that a reported finding reflects a genuinely broad, real improvement rather than an artifact of overfitting, whether deliberate or accidental, to one particular benchmark's own idiosyncrasies.

question: Andina Retail Group's AI researcher reports not just a technique's average benchmark performance but also its performance variance across multiple independent runs (different random initializations, for instance). Why does reporting this variance, not just the average, matter for a genuinely informative benchmark evaluation, connecting to the confidence-interval reasoning already covered in this course?

options.a: Two techniques can share the exact same average benchmark performance while genuinely differing in how consistently reliable that performance actually is across repeated runs — a technique with high variance might occasionally perform excellently and occasionally quite poorly, which matters enormously for real deployment reliability, even though its average looks identical to a consistently mediocre-but-stable alternative; reporting variance alongside the average, echoing the confidence-interval reasoning from air-026, gives a genuinely more complete picture of a technique's real, practical reliability
options.b: Reporting performance variance across independent runs has no relationship to the confidence-interval reasoning already covered earlier in this course
options.c: A technique with high variance across independent runs is always strictly better for real deployment than a technique with low variance and the same average performance
options.d: A technique's average benchmark performance always fully and completely captures everything genuinely relevant about its reliability, making variance across independent runs an irrelevant additional statistic

theory.text: This closes the entire benchmarks-evaluation-methodology topic by connecting directly to the confidence-interval reasoning already established in air-026, here applied specifically to run-to-run variance in benchmark evaluation. Two techniques can report the exact same average performance while genuinely differing in something that matters a great deal for real deployment: how consistently that performance actually holds up across repeated runs. A technique with high variance might occasionally perform excellently and occasionally quite poorly depending on essentially arbitrary factors like random initialization, even though its average looks identical to a different technique that performs consistently, reliably in the middle every time — for a real business deciding whether to actually deploy a technique, that consistency difference matters enormously, and it's completely invisible if only the average is ever reported, exactly why the confidence-interval discipline from air-026 extends naturally to reporting variance across independent runs, not just a single point estimate of average benchmark performance.

question: Andina Retail Group's AI researcher distinguishes between a result being 'statistically significant' and a result being 'practically significant' — noting that a very large sample size can make even a tiny, practically meaningless improvement register as statistically significant. Why is this distinction genuinely important for honest research communication?

options.a: A large sample size always makes a genuinely tiny, practically meaningless improvement statistically insignificant, the reverse of the actual typical relationship between sample size and statistical significance
options.b: With a large enough sample size, even a genuinely tiny improvement (a 0.01% accuracy gain, for instance) can register as statistically significant, meaning the improvement is unlikely to be purely due to chance — but statistical significance alone says nothing about whether that improvement is actually large enough to matter for any real, practical business purpose; a researcher must separately assess practical significance (whether the improvement is meaningfully large enough to justify a real decision) rather than treating statistical significance alone as proof a finding is genuinely important
options.c: This concept has no relationship to the confidence-interval or multiple-comparisons-correction concepts already covered earlier in this course
options.d: A statistically significant result is always automatically a practically significant result as well, making it unnecessary to ever separately assess whether a finding is large enough to matter for a real business decision

theory.text: This extends the statistical rigor topic with a genuinely important distinction that complements the confidence-interval reasoning from air-026 and the multiple-comparisons correction from air-025. Statistical significance answers one specific, narrow question: is this improvement unlikely to be purely due to random chance, given the sample size used? With a large enough sample, even a genuinely tiny, practically meaningless improvement — a 0.01% accuracy gain that would never actually change any real business decision — can register as statistically significant, since a large sample makes even small true effects detectable with confidence. But statistical significance alone says absolutely nothing about whether that improvement is actually large enough to matter for any real, practical purpose — a researcher must separately, honestly assess practical significance, asking directly whether the improvement is meaningfully large enough to justify a real decision, rather than treating the presence of statistical significance alone as automatic proof that a finding is genuinely important or worth acting on.

question: Andina Retail Group's AI researcher, before running a new experiment, calculates the minimum sample size needed to reliably detect an improvement of a practically meaningful size (a 'power analysis'), rather than simply using whatever sample happens to already be available. Why does this kind of advance power analysis reflect sound statistical practice?

options.a: An experiment run with too small a sample size may lack the statistical power to reliably detect a genuine, practically meaningful improvement even if that improvement actually exists — producing a misleading 'no significant difference' result that reflects an underpowered experiment rather than a genuine absence of any real effect; calculating the needed sample size in advance, based on the smallest improvement size that would actually matter practically, helps ensure the experiment is actually capable of detecting a genuine effect if one exists, rather than risking a false negative purely due to insufficient sample size
options.b: An experiment's sample size has no relationship to its ability to reliably detect a genuine, practically meaningful improvement, making advance power analysis an unnecessary statistical exercise
options.c: A power analysis conducted before running an experiment always guarantees the experiment will find a statistically significant result, regardless of whether a genuine effect actually exists
options.d: This concept has no relationship to the practical-versus-statistical-significance distinction already covered in the previous question

theory.text: This closes the entire statistical-rigor-significance topic by addressing the reverse risk from the one raised in air-041. Where air-041 warned that a large sample can make a practically meaningless improvement look statistically significant, this addresses the opposite problem: a sample size that's too small can fail to reliably detect a genuine, practically meaningful improvement even when that improvement actually, truly exists — producing a misleading 'no significant difference' conclusion that really just reflects an underpowered experiment, not a genuine absence of any real effect. Calculating the minimum sample size needed in advance, based explicitly on the smallest improvement size that would actually matter practically (directly connecting to the practical-significance distinction from air-041), helps ensure an experiment is genuinely capable of detecting a real effect if one exists, rather than risking a false negative conclusion purely because the experiment was never actually large enough to reliably notice a genuine improvement in the first place.

question: Andina Retail Group's AI researcher archives the exact software library versions used during an experiment (via a dependency lock file), not just the code itself, alongside a published research finding. Why does pinning exact dependency versions matter for genuine long-term reproducibility, beyond simply sharing the analysis code?

options.a: Sharing only the analysis code, with no record of the exact library versions used, is always exactly as sufficient for genuine reproducibility as also archiving the exact dependency versions
options.b: Software libraries change behavior over time as they're updated — a bug fix, a changed default parameter, a different underlying numerical algorithm — meaning the exact same analysis code can produce a genuinely different result when run against a different library version than the one originally used; archiving the exact dependency versions alongside the code ensures a future researcher attempting to reproduce the finding is actually running the same computational environment, not just the same code operating on a potentially different, silently changed foundation
options.c: Software library versions never affect an analysis's actual output, making it unnecessary to archive which specific versions were used alongside a published finding
options.d: This concept has no relationship to the reproducibility documentation or data-card concepts already covered earlier in this course

theory.text: This extends the reproducibility documentation discipline from air-012 and the data-card concept from air-028 to a subtler, easily overlooked reproducibility risk: the software environment itself, not just the code or the data. Software libraries genuinely change behavior over time as they're updated — a bug fix that changes a numerical result, an updated default parameter, a revised underlying algorithm — meaning literally the exact same analysis code, run against a different version of an underlying library than originally used, can genuinely produce a different result, through no error in the code itself at all. Archiving the exact dependency versions used (via a lock file recording precise version numbers, not just library names) alongside the published code ensures a future researcher attempting a genuine reproduction is actually running the same full computational environment the original finding depended on, not just the same code sitting on top of a potentially different, silently changed foundation that could itself explain any discrepancy in the reproduction attempt.

question: Andina Retail Group's AI researcher, when sharing a trained model publicly, includes clear documentation of the specific version of the training data used, not just the final trained model itself. Why does documenting the specific training-data version matter, even when the model itself is fully shared and available?

options.a: A trained model shared publicly always fully and completely reveals everything about the specific data it was trained on, making separate training-data documentation an unnecessary redundant step
options.b: This concept has no relationship to the data-card documentation concept already covered earlier in this course
options.c: A shared trained model alone doesn't reveal what specific data it was actually trained on, or when that data was collected — without this documentation, a future researcher can't properly assess whether the model's training data might be outdated relative to a new use case, might carry a specific collection-time bias, or might make the model's behavior on new data genuinely difficult to interpret or trust; documenting the specific training-data version gives future users the same kind of essential context the data-card concept already established for data being genuinely usable and interpretable
options.d: The specific version of training data used to train a model has no relationship to how confidently a future researcher can interpret or trust that model's behavior on new data

theory.text: This closes the entire reproducibility-open-science topic by extending the data-card documentation concept from air-028 to the specific case of sharing a trained model, not just a raw dataset. A shared trained model, however openly and fully released, is essentially a black box about its own history unless accompanied by explicit documentation of what specific data version it was actually trained on and when that data was collected — without this, a future researcher genuinely can't assess whether the training data might now be outdated relative to their own intended new use case, whether it might carry a bias specific to when and how it was originally collected, or how much to actually trust the model's behavior on data that looks meaningfully different from what it originally saw during training. Documenting the specific training-data version gives future users of a shared model the same essential interpretive context the data-card concept already established as necessary for any shared data artifact to be genuinely usable and trustworthy, closing this topic's consistent throughline that transparency about origins, not just transparency about final outputs, is what genuine reproducibility actually requires.

question: Andina Retail Group's AI researcher, when a research project involves analyzing real customer data, ensures the specific research use case falls within what customers actually consented to when that data was originally collected, rather than assuming any internally-available data is automatically fair to use for any internal research purpose. Why does checking consent scope specifically for the research use case matter, even for data the company already legitimately possesses?

options.a: This concept has no relationship to the independent ethical review already covered earlier in this course
options.b: A customer's original consent to data collection is typically given for a specific, disclosed purpose — legitimately possessing data within the company doesn't automatically extend that original consent to cover every possible additional internal research use it might later be put to; checking that a specific new research use case genuinely falls within what customers actually agreed to respects the customer's actual informed choice, rather than treating mere internal data availability as sufficient justification on its own
options.c: Data the company already legitimately possesses can always be used for any internal research purpose whatsoever, regardless of what customers originally consented to when the data was first collected
options.d: Checking whether a specific research use case falls within a customer's original data consent has no relationship to genuine research ethics practice

theory.text: This extends the research ethics topic with a foundational consent-scope discipline that connects directly to the independent-review value from air-029. A customer's original consent to having their data collected is typically given for a specific, disclosed purpose at the time of collection — the company legitimately possessing that data internally doesn't automatically mean every possible future internal research use is equally covered by that original consent. Checking explicitly that a new, specific research use case genuinely falls within what customers actually agreed to when the data was first collected respects the customer's actual informed choice about how their own data gets used, rather than treating mere internal data availability as sufficient justification on its own — exactly the kind of concern an independent ethical reviewer, without the original researcher's proximity to the project, would be well positioned to catch if the original researcher's own closeness to the work caused them to assume availability alone was enough.

question: Andina Retail Group's AI researcher, when a research finding could plausibly be used both to genuinely improve customer service and to enable a more invasive, potentially unwelcome form of customer monitoring, explicitly documents this dual-use tension in the research write-up rather than presenting only the beneficial application. Why does explicitly surfacing this kind of dual-use tension matter, even when the researcher's own intended application is genuinely the beneficial one?

options.a: A research finding with a genuinely beneficial intended application can never plausibly be applied in any other, less beneficial way, making dual-use documentation an unnecessary consideration
options.b: A research finding, once published or shared internally, can potentially be applied by others in ways well beyond the researcher's own original, well-intentioned purpose — explicitly documenting a genuine dual-use tension gives whoever later decides how to actually apply the finding the honest context needed to make that decision deliberately and responsibly, rather than the researcher's own good intentions silently standing in for a decision that others, with different incentives, might make quite differently once they encounter the same finding
options.c: Documenting a dual-use tension in a research write-up means the researcher believes the beneficial application should never actually be pursued at all
options.d: This concept has no relationship to the dual-use disclosure considerations already covered earlier in this course

theory.text: This closes the entire research-ethics-ai topic by extending the dual-use disclosure principle already referenced earlier in this course with a concrete Andina Retail Group scenario. A research finding, once actually published or shared internally, doesn't stay tethered to only the researcher's own original, well-intentioned application — it can be picked up and applied by others, later, with different incentives and different priorities, in ways the original researcher never intended or would have chosen themselves. Explicitly documenting a genuine dual-use tension when it exists — here, a finding that could serve genuinely improved customer service or a more invasive, less welcome form of customer monitoring — gives whoever later actually decides how to apply the finding the honest context needed to make that decision deliberately and responsibly, rather than letting the original researcher's own good intentions silently substitute for a decision that someone else, encountering the same finding under different pressures, might make quite differently without that documented context available to inform their choice.

question: Andina Retail Group's AI researcher, when presenting a research finding, explicitly distinguishes between what the data actually shows and the researcher's own interpretation or recommendation built on top of that data, rather than presenting both as a single, undifferentiated conclusion. Why does keeping this distinction clear matter for honest research communication?

options.a: An audience should never be given the opportunity to evaluate a researcher's interpretation separately from the underlying data finding itself, since only the researcher is qualified to judge whether the interpretation is sound
options.b: The raw finding (what the data actually shows) and the researcher's interpretation or recommendation (a judgment call built on top of that finding, potentially incorporating assumptions or business context beyond the data itself) are genuinely different kinds of claims, carrying different kinds of uncertainty — keeping them clearly distinguished lets an audience separately evaluate whether they trust the underlying data and whether they agree with the researcher's specific interpretation or recommended action, rather than being forced to accept or reject both together as a single inseparable package
options.c: This concept has no relationship to the audience-tailored communication or limitations-disclosure principles already covered earlier in this course
options.d: A research finding and a researcher's interpretation or recommendation built on top of that finding are always identical in their level of certainty, making any distinction between them unnecessary for honest communication

theory.text: This extends the honest-communication discipline from air-016 and air-031 with a further, genuinely important distinction. What the data actually shows (the raw finding) and what the researcher recommends doing about it (an interpretation that may reasonably incorporate business judgment, assumptions, or context beyond the data itself) are genuinely different kinds of claims, carrying different kinds of uncertainty — the data finding might be quite solid and well-supported, while the specific recommended action built on top of it involves additional judgment calls reasonable people might weigh differently. Keeping these two things clearly, explicitly distinguished in a research communication lets an audience separately evaluate whether they trust the underlying data and whether they independently agree with the researcher's specific interpretation or recommended course of action, rather than being forced to accept or reject the whole package together as if the finding and the recommendation carried exactly the same level of certainty and the same kind of justification.

question: Andina Retail Group's AI researcher, when a leadership audience asks a follow-up question the current research doesn't actually answer, explicitly says so rather than extrapolating an answer that sounds plausible but goes beyond what the actual research supports. Why does this kind of honest 'the research doesn't actually address that' response reflect sound research communication practice, even when it might feel less satisfying to the audience than confidently offering some answer?

options.a: Acknowledging that current research doesn't address a specific follow-up question always makes the entire research effort appear worthless and not worth having conducted in the first place
options.b: A researcher should always confidently offer some plausible-sounding answer to any leadership follow-up question, regardless of whether the actual underlying research genuinely supports that specific answer
options.c: This concept has no relationship to the limitations-disclosure principle already covered earlier in this course
options.d: Extrapolating a plausible-sounding answer beyond what the actual research genuinely supports risks the audience treating that extrapolation with the same confidence as the actual, well-supported finding, since the distinction between genuinely established and merely plausible-sounding often isn't obvious to a non-technical audience in the moment — explicitly acknowledging a genuine limit of what the current research actually addresses protects the audience from unknowingly acting on a confident-sounding guess as if it were an established, well-supported result

theory.text: This closes the entire communicating-research topic, and the entire AI Research Methods course's core content, by extending the limitations-disclosure principle from air-031 to the specific, common real-world moment of an unscripted leadership follow-up question. Extrapolating a plausible-sounding answer that goes beyond what the actual research genuinely supports carries a real risk: the audience, especially a non-technical one, often can't easily distinguish a genuinely well-supported finding from a confident-sounding but actually unsupported extrapolation offered in the moment — both can sound equally authoritative coming from the same researcher in the same presentation. Explicitly acknowledging a genuine limit — 'the current research doesn't actually address that specific question' — protects the audience from unknowingly acting on what amounts to an educated guess as if it carried the same evidentiary weight as the actual, rigorously supported finding, honoring this entire course's consistent throughline that genuine research value depends on honest calibration between what's actually known and what merely sounds plausible in the moment.

question: Andina Retail Group's AI researcher structures a research presentation to leadership by leading with the practical implication and recommended action first, then following with the supporting evidence and methodology for those who want more detail, rather than building up to the conclusion only at the very end after walking through the full methodology first. Why does this 'conclusion first' structure often communicate more effectively to a busy leadership audience than a methodology-first structure?

options.a: A research presentation should always follow the exact chronological order in which the underlying research work was actually performed, regardless of what structure would actually best serve a specific audience's needs
options.b: A methodology-first presentation structure and a conclusion-first presentation structure always communicate a research finding exactly equally effectively to a busy leadership audience with genuinely limited time and attention
options.c: A busy leadership audience typically needs to grasp the practical implication and recommended action quickly to make good use of a limited amount of attention and time; leading with that conclusion first, then offering the supporting methodology and evidence for whoever wants to dig deeper, respects that reality — a methodology-first structure risks losing a time-constrained audience's attention before the actually decision-relevant conclusion is ever reached, echoing this course's consistent theme that communication should be structured around what a specific audience actually needs, not around the order the underlying work was actually performed
options.d: This concept has no relationship to the audience-tailored communication or visualization-based cognitive-load principles already covered earlier in this course

theory.text: This closes the entire communicating-research topic by tying presentation structure itself back to the audience-tailored communication theme running throughout this whole topic (air-016, air-031, air-032, air-047, air-048). A busy leadership audience typically has genuinely limited time and attention to devote to any single research presentation, and what they most need to walk away with is the practical implication and recommended action — leading with that conclusion first, then offering the supporting methodology and evidence afterward for anyone who genuinely wants to dig deeper, respects that reality directly. A methodology-first structure, however methodologically thorough and honest, risks losing a time-constrained audience's attention before the actually decision-relevant conclusion is ever reached at the very end — reinforcing this course's consistent closing theme that genuinely effective research communication is structured around what a specific audience actually needs in order to understand and act on a finding, not around the chronological order in which the underlying research work happened to be performed.

question: Andina Retail Group's AI researcher, when a new experimental result seems surprisingly strong, deliberately tries harder to find a flaw in the experimental setup before accepting the result at face value, rather than accepting an unexpectedly favorable result more readily than an unexpectedly unfavorable one. Why does applying extra scrutiny specifically to surprisingly favorable results, not just unfavorable ones, reflect sound experimental discipline?

options.a: There's a natural human tendency to scrutinize a disappointing or unfavorable result closely while accepting a pleasantly surprising, favorable result more readily and with less scrutiny — but an experimental flaw (a data leak, a bug, an unintended confound) is exactly as likely to produce a surprisingly good-looking result as a surprisingly bad one, so applying at least as much scrutiny to unexpectedly favorable results as to unfavorable ones, rather than less, guards against exactly the kind of asymmetric bias that can let a genuinely broken experimental setup masquerade as a genuine breakthrough
options.b: This concept has no relationship to the data-leakage or sanity-check-baseline discipline already covered earlier in this course
options.c: Experimental flaws like data leakage or setup bugs can only ever produce disappointing, unfavorable-looking results, never a surprisingly strong, favorable-looking one
options.d: A surprisingly strong experimental result should always be accepted at face value with no additional scrutiny, since a favorable outcome is inherently less likely to reflect an underlying experimental flaw than an unfavorable one

theory.text: This adds a final, genuinely important discipline to the experimental-design-ai topic, tying together the data-leakage check from air-022 and the sanity-check-baseline reasoning from air-021 into a broader mindset. There's a natural, well-documented human tendency to scrutinize a disappointing or unfavorable result closely, looking hard for what might have gone wrong, while accepting a pleasantly surprising, favorable result far more readily, with considerably less scrutiny applied before it gets accepted and reported. But an experimental flaw like data leakage, an evaluation bug, or an unintended confound is exactly as capable of producing a surprisingly good-looking result as a surprisingly bad one — arguably more capable, since these are precisely the kinds of errors that artificially inflate apparent performance. Applying at least as much rigorous scrutiny to an unexpectedly favorable result as to an unfavorable one, rather than the natural asymmetric instinct to accept good news more readily, guards directly against a genuinely broken experimental setup quietly masquerading as a genuine, exciting breakthrough — closing this course's experimental-design content with the same rigorous, self-skeptical discipline it has emphasized throughout, applied here to the researcher's own psychology, not just to the technical experimental setup itself.
